questions.question: Explain backshift in your own words.
questions.sampleAnswer: Backshift is the rule that the tense of a reported sentence shifts one step into the past compared to the original direct speech — present becomes past, past becomes past perfect, will becomes would. This signals that the words are quoted rather than spoken right now.

questions.question: Convert to reported speech: 'I am hungry,' Tom said.
questions.sampleAnswer: Tom said (that) he was hungry. Pronoun: I → he. Tense backshift: am → was.

questions.question: Convert: 'I will help you tomorrow,' she said.
questions.sampleAnswer: She said (that) she would help me the next day. Pronouns: I → she, you → me. Modal backshift: will → would. Time word: tomorrow → the next/following day.

questions.question: Why does the time word 'now' become 'then' in reported speech?
questions.sampleAnswer: When you report what someone said, you take a different position in time. 'Now' was the moment of the original speech, but at the moment of reporting that's already over — so it becomes 'then', referring back to that earlier time. The same logic applies to 'today/tomorrow/yesterday/here'.

questions.question: When can you skip the backshift? Give an example.
questions.sampleAnswer: You skip the backshift when the reporting verb is in the present tense (She says she is tired — kein backshift) or when the statement is still a general truth (He said the earth is round). In both cases the original tense remains.

questions.question: Convert: 'I have lost my keys here,' Anna told me yesterday.
questions.sampleAnswer: Anna told me (that) she had lost her keys there. Backshift: have lost → had lost. Pronoun: I → she, my → her. Place: here → there.

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'say' and 'tell'.
questions.sampleAnswer: After 'tell' you always need a person (tell me, tell him, tell us). After 'say' the recipient is optional and, if present, must be introduced with 'to' (say to me, say to him). So: He told me a story / He said hello to me — never 'He told a story to me' or 'He said me a story'.

questions.question: Why do we use precise reporting verbs like 'admit' or 'warn' instead of always using 'say'?
questions.sampleAnswer: Precise reporting verbs add information about the speaker's intention or attitude. 'Say' just reports words; 'admit' shows the speaker confessed something they didn't want to, 'warn' signals danger, 'suggest' is a proposal. Using these verbs makes your English more natural and shows that you understand the speech act, not just the words.

questions.question: Convert 'I'll definitely call you back' (he to her) using a more precise verb than 'said'.
questions.sampleAnswer: He promised to call her back. 'Promise' is more precise than 'said' because it captures the speech act — giving one's word.

questions.question: Convert 'Don't touch the wires!' as a reported warning.
questions.sampleAnswer: He warned us not to touch the wires. Structure: warn + person + (not) to + infinitive.

questions.question: Convert with the most precise verb: 'Yes, I broke the window. I'm sorry.' (Tim to mum)
questions.sampleAnswer: Tim admitted to his mum that he had broken the window and apologised. 'Admit' captures the confession and 'apologise' adds the regret — that's much more informative than just 'said'.

questions.question: Why does 'tell' need a person but 'say' doesn't? Where does this come from logically?
questions.sampleAnswer: Logically, 'tell' is about communication — you transmit information to someone, so an audience is needed. 'Say' is more about producing words — you can say something into the air without a specific recipient. This is why 'tell' is incomplete without 'me/him/us', but 'say' can stand alone or take 'to + person' as an optional addition.

questions.question: Find the mistake: 'She asked me where do I work.'
options: 'do I work' should be 'I worked' or 'I work' (statement order)
'where' should be 'whether'
no mistake
'asked me' should be 'told me'

questions.question: Find the mistake: 'He asked me did I know the answer.'
options: 'did I know' should be 'I knew' or 'if I knew'
'asked' should be 'said'
no mistake
'me' should be 'to me'

questions.question: Most natural sentence:
options: She asked me what was my favourite colour.
She asked me what my favourite colour was.
She asked me what is my favourite colour.
She asked me what my favourite colour is.

questions.question: Most natural sentence:
options: He told me to be quiet please.
He asked me to be quiet.
He said me to be quiet.
He said to be quiet to me.

questions.question: Best reported version of: 'Could you possibly turn down the music?' (very polite request)
options: He told us to turn down the music.
He asked us to turn down the music.
He ordered us to turn down the music.
He begged us to turn down the music.

questions.question: Best reported version of: 'GET OUT NOW!!!' (yelling)
options: He asked us to get out.
He told us to get out.
He ordered us to get out immediately.
He suggested getting out.

questions.question: Why doesn't an indirect question take a question mark?
questions.sampleAnswer: Because an indirect question is a statement about a question, not the question itself. The reporter is telling someone what was asked — that's a statement, so it ends with a full stop. The question mark belongs only to a direct question.

questions.question: Convert: 'Where do you live?' she asked me.
questions.sampleAnswer: She asked me where I lived. Question word 'where' stays. Word order changes from question (do you live) to statement (I lived). No 'do/does/did' in indirect questions. Backshift: live → lived.

questions.question: Convert this Yes/No-question: 'Did you see my keys?' she asked Tom.
questions.sampleAnswer: She asked Tom if he had seen her keys. Yes/No-question → 'if' (or 'whether'). Word order: subject before verb (he had seen). 'Did see' → 'had seen' (backshift). Pronoun: my → her.

questions.question: Convert this imperative: 'Don't touch the wires!' (he to us)
questions.sampleAnswer: He told us not to touch the wires. Or more precisely: He warned us not to touch the wires. Form: tell/warn + person + not + to + infinitive. Imperatives never become 'that'-clauses.

questions.question: List the four typical mistakes students make when reporting questions.
questions.sampleAnswer: 1) Keeping the question mark in an indirect question — should be a full stop. 2) Keeping question word order (Where do you live) instead of statement order (where I live). 3) Carrying over 'do/does/did' — these are only used for direct question form, not in indirect. 4) Forgetting to use 'if' or 'whether' for Yes/No-questions.

questions.question: When would you choose 'order' rather than 'tell' as a reporting verb for a command? Give an example.
questions.sampleAnswer: 'Order' is used when the speaker has clear authority and the command is strict — typically a police officer, military officer, judge, or boss. Example: 'Drop your weapon!' said the police officer → The police officer ordered the suspect to drop his weapon. 'Told' would be too neutral for the strong authority of the command.
